notes.anchor: we should achieve just so much glory and triumph as is necessary
notes.note: A triumph, the formal victory-parade voted by the Senate, required a real military success; Caelius wishes Cicero a war just big enough to earn one without the genuine danger of a full Parthian invasion.

notes.anchor: if the Parthian moves at all
notes.note: After the Roman disaster at Carrhae (53 BC) a Parthian counter-invasion of Syria and Cilicia was the standing fear of Cicero's governorship; his single under-strength army could not have held the passes.
The tribunician veto (intercessio): any tribune of the plebs could block a senatorial decree or motion by his single 'no,' the procedural lever that kept the provinces question deadlocked for years.
The unresolved question of replacing Caesar in his Gallic command; until it was settled no successor could be named to Cicero in Cilicia either, so the deadlock pinned Cicero in his province.

notes.anchor: If, with this year passed by, Curio is to be tribune
notes.note: C. Scribonius Curio's tribunate began in December 50 BC; Caelius foresees that he will reopen the whole provinces question, as in fact he did, helping to precipitate the civil war.
